courseTitle: Speak Algerian Arabic: Quick, Natural and Rooted
courseBrief:
Create a cumulative self-study course for English-speaking adult beginners, progressing toward independent conversation in Algerian Darja. Use a broadly useful central urban baseline associated mainly with Algiers without presenting it as the sole or universally preferred Algerian variety. Productive forms must be credible and consistent; important western, eastern, Saharan and Amazigh-influenced differences should appear as bounded recognition notes using cautious language such as “you may hear.” Distinguish ordinary Darja, Modern Standard Arabic, integrated French-derived vocabulary, active French code-switching, Amazigh-origin or influenced forms, and regional or generational usage.

Teach approximately 380 productive and 150 recognition items through a compact, structurally connected sentence engine rather than disconnected lists. A typical chapter adds 10–16 productive items selected for conversational utility, combinability, recurrence, pronunciation value, cultural value and manageable learning cost. Mark productive versus recognition vocabulary. Recycle productive language within its chapter, elsewhere in its section, in review or listening work and in the section exit task. Introduce past and future early enough for genuine conversation, and prioritise clarification, repair, connected speech and realistic code-switching.

Every chapter needs a communicative outcome and realistic situation; productive and recognition vocabulary; reusable sentence frames; accessible and ordinary-speed dialogues; Arabic script, consistent pedagogical transliteration and natural English; pronunciation or rhythm work; pragmatic guidance; bounded regional recognition; relevant cultural or historical material; substitution and retrieval practice; listening work; an information-gap or personalised speaking task; TTS-ready scripts and recording notes; sources and native-review questions. Dialogues must accomplish believable purposes rather than merely display vocabulary. Script learning must support speaking and must not delay it. Keep pedagogical transliteration distinct from informal Arabizi.

Base linguistic, regional, cultural, culinary, musical and historical claims on credible sources. Prefer corpora and documented recordings, peer-reviewed work, Algerian scholars and universities, institutional cultural sources, specialist dictionaries and teaching materials, followed by native-speaker elicitation. Informal examples establish occurrence only. Search in English, French and Arabic and, where useful, test Arabic spellings, spelling variants and Arabizi. Cite researched claims inline and provide source lists. Flag productive language that still requires native-speaker validation. Never invent proverbs, etymologies, quotations, lyrics or causal historical claims, and do not reproduce copyrighted lyrics.

Use colonial history only when a documented mechanism connects it to language hierarchy, education, oral transmission, names and places, solidarity, food continuity, music, humour, resistance, migration, women’s work or multilingual appropriation. Separate documented events, personal memories, collective narratives and editorial interpretation. Seek Algerian and independent scholarly or primary evidence for sensitive claims. Represent ordinary life, pleasure, creativity and contemporary ambition alongside suffering and resistance; avoid stereotypes, romanticisation and treating one family account as the national story.

The project produces audio scripts and manifests, not final audio. Important utterances should support a careful phrase, natural phrase, shadowing groups and a dialogue response or contrast. TTS may prototype vocabulary, substitution and slow review. Prefer native recording for anchor dialogues, regional comparison, code-switching, humour, hospitality, music-influenced speech and sensitive history. Every audio item must specify clip ID, Arabic text, transliteration, meaning, proposed speaker region/profile, relationship and setting, pace, emotional intent, pauses, code-switching, pronunciation risks, TTS acceptability and whether human recording is required. Never direct generic TTS to fabricate an unsupported regional accent.

Editors must control vocabulary growth, preserve cumulative progression, reconcile terminology and transliteration, retain evidence limits and produce practical, readable sections rather than linguistic monographs. The finished course contains all nine sections and forty-nine chapters plus a concise introduction and usable appendices.

tasks.title: Course foundation: method, dialect, notation, evidence and audio
tasks.instructions: Establish the executable course-wide production standard. Define the compact sentence-engine teaching method; central urban/Algiers baseline and bounded regional policy; distinctions among Darja, MSA, French-derived words, active French code-switching, Amazigh influence and regional/generational forms; Arabic-script conventions; one consistent pedagogical transliteration system; Arabizi treatment; pronunciation notation; productive/recognition labels; approximate 380/150 vocabulary budget and section allocations; recurrence rules; cumulative grammar sequence; native-validation flags; the complete chapter and section templates; exercise and section-exit design; inline citation and source-quality policy; handling of uncertainty and sensitive colonial history; and TTS/recording conventions. Specify an audio manifest schema containing every field required by the brief and stable clip-ID conventions. Include a proposed cross-course lexical ledger structure so independently drafted chapters can avoid obvious duplication. Research and cite linguistic choices; identify decisions requiring native review. Do not draft course chapters.
researchQueries: Algerian Arabic Algiers dialect phonology grammar corpus pedagogical transliteration
arabe algérien parler d’Alger phonétique corpus transcription variation régionale
اللهجة الجزائرية لهجة العاصمة الأصوات التفريغ المدونات الاختلافات الجهوية

tasks.title: Introduction — How to use Speak Algerian Arabic
tasks.instructions: Using the foundation, write the concise learner-facing introduction. Explain the sentence-engine method, productive versus recognition vocabulary, central baseline and regional limits, Arabic script and transliteration, careful versus natural audio, shadowing, retrieval practice, code-switching labels, native-review flags and respectful use of cultural and historical material. Give a practical study routine for book, web and audio use without teaching a full chapter or making unsupported factual claims. Include citations only for factual claims that need them.

tasks.title: Chapter 1 draft — What Algerian Arabic sounds like
tasks.instructions: Draft Chapter 1 on Algerian rhythm, reduced vowels, connected words, essential consonants, careful versus rapid speech, Arabic script, pedagogical transliteration, Arabizi and a bounded regional pronunciation preview. Frame Darja as an oral, adaptive language rather than deficient MSA. Follow the foundation. Include a communicative outcome and realistic situation; 10–16 productive items where appropriate plus clearly separated recognition vocabulary; reusable sentence frames; purposeful accessible and ordinary-speech dialogues; every taught utterance in Arabic script, consistent transliteration and natural English; pronunciation/rhythm instruction; pragmatic guidance; bounded regional notes; relevant cultural/history material only; substitution, retrieval, listening and personalised or information-gap tasks; deliberate recycling; TTS-ready scripts and a full recording manifest; inline citations and source list for research; and precise native-review questions and validation flags.
